title: Total greenhouse gas emissions, excluding land use and forestry
subtitle: [Greenhouse gas emissions](#dod:ghgemissions) are measured in tonnes of [carbon dioxide-equivalents](#dod:carbondioxideequivalents) per person. Contributions from [land-use change](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) and forestry are not included.
caption:
Total greenhouse gas emissions, excluding land use and forestry

Greenhouse gas emissions are measured in tonnes of carbon dioxide-equivalents per person. Contributions from land-use change and forestry are not included.

This choropleth world map displays total greenhouse gas emissions for the year 2024, excluding land use and forestry. The legend at the bottom defines the color scale using absolute emission values in tonnes, ranging from 0 to 10 billion tonnes, with specific thresholds marked at 10 million, 30 million, 100 million, 300 million, 1 billion, and 3 billion tonnes. The darkest green band represents the highest emission levels, clearly occupied by China and the United States, which both sit in the range extending toward 10 billion tonnes. India appears in a slightly lighter but still dark green shade, placing it in the band between 1 billion and 3 billion tonnes. Russia and Brazil are visible in mid-range green shades that correspond to the 300 million to 1 billion tonne interval. Much of Europe, including Germany, France, and the United Kingdom, along with Canada and Australia, sit in lighter green bands that fall between the 100 million and 300 million tonne marks. Large portions of Africa, including the Sahara region, and parts of South America appear in the lightest yellow-green shades, indicating values near the lower end of the scale, often below 30 million tonnes. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching, signifying no data is available for that territory. The visual distribution highlights a concentration of the highest absolute emissions in East Asia and North America, while vast areas of the Global South display lighter shades corresponding to lower total emission figures.
